scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a food-and-beverage ingredient-specific handoff package with explicit ownership, lot-aware artifacts, COA context, and blocker state.
Missing approved-supplier, shelf-life, and destination-acceptance prerequisites were treated as blockers rather than a routable handoff.
Duplicate ownership and conflicting schedules were escalated with an owner map instead of dropped into generic coordination.
Conflicting ERP, MES, WMS, and QMS ingredient-lot records were preserved as a traceability blocker and not collapsed into a confident answer.
Quality-hold posture was preserved and formal release authority stayed with quality owners.
Supplier and substitute-ingredient approval was rejected and routed to supplier-quality and formulation or change-control owners.
The lane refused to absorb PO, freight, release, and shortage-closure execution work and returned boundary-safe routing guidance.
Missing tenant authority, allergen, and handoff maps correctly triggered a low-confidence shortage or vendor-risk escalation.
Conflicting public and tenant quarantine or rework rules were escalated for human resolution instead of synthesized into a definitive instruction.
Ingredient-specific terms were normalized into authoritative artifact and status states before routing.
Closure was only confirmed after destination-owner proof and required artifact completeness were explicit.
Supplier nonconformance was escalated with throughput risk preserved and without unsupported continuation.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to ingredient supply continuity, approved-supplier and COA evidence, hold boundaries, and owner routing.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the issue contract.
